claims.claim_text: Knight Capital suffered a major automated trading incident on August 1, 2012.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening lines
claims.rationale: SEC enforcement materials document the August 1, 2012 Knight Capital incident, but sec.gov is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module gives exact timing, throughput, loss, and corporate-outcome details for the Knight Capital incident.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: For exactly 45 minutes, their automated system executed trades at a massive rate of 40 orders per second—erroneously buying high and selling low. By 10:15 AM, the company had hemorrhaged $440 million. By the end of the week, Knight Capital had to be sold off in pieces to survive.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For roughly 45 minutes, the faulty rollout flooded the market with unintended orders and caused losses severe enough to threaten the firm's survival.
claims.rationale: This sentence mixes a real incident with unsupported or inaccurate precision, so the false specifics should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The root cause involved a partial deployment to one of eight SMARS servers, which left legacy Power Peg logic active behind a repurposed flag.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: The SEC order directly describes the one-of-eight-server deployment failure and legacy-code activation, but sec.gov is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Knight Capital had no automated deployment pipeline, no environment consistency checks, and no immediate automated rollback mechanism.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: There was no automated deployment pipeline, no environment consistency checks, and absolutely no mechanism for immediate automated rollback.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The incident exposed serious gaps in deployment controls, environment consistency checks, and incident response.
claims.rationale: The underlying control failures are real, but this sentence overstates them with absolutes that the primary materials do not directly support.

claims.claim_text: Sculley et al.'s 2015 paper argued that mature ML systems may be at most 5% ML code and at least 95% surrounding glue code.
claims.span_hint: section: The Complexity Explosion, Did You Know callout
claims.rationale: The paper contains this exact 5%/95% observation, but the clean paper host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A 2019 McGill study found that only 6% of ML papers could be fully reproduced because of missing hyperparameters and unreported random seeds.
claims.span_hint: section: The Three Pillars of ML Version Control, Did You Know callout
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** In a 2019 study, researchers at McGill University found that only 6% of machine learning papers could be fully reproduced due to missing hyperparameters and unreported random seeds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Reproducing published ML results becomes much harder when papers omit key details such as hyperparameters, random seeds, data-processing steps, or environment settings.
claims.rationale: I could not verify this exact McGill-attributed 6% figure and causal explanation from an authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: DVC keeps version information in Git while storing data and model artifacts outside Git, and it supports reproducible ML pipelines.
claims.span_hint: section: Data Version Control (DVC): Git for Your Data, paragraph under 'DVC: The Solution'
claims.rationale: The DVC README directly explains these core product capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Algorithmia's 2022 State of ML survey found that 55% of companies had never deployed a single ML model to production because of weak MLOps practices.
claims.span_hint: section: Testing Strategies for ML Code, Did You Know callout after Model Robustness
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** Algorithmia's 2022 State of ML survey found that 55% of companies have never deployed a single ML model to production due to a lack of robust MLOps practices. Testing pipelines build the organizational trust required to release.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Many organizations struggle to move ML models into production, which is one reason teams invest in repeatable testing, deployment, and monitoring practices.
claims.rationale: An off-list Algorithmia report contains a similar 55% figure, but the module's year/title are wrong and the causal attribution is overstated.

claims.claim_text: GitHub secret scanning detected over 700,000 exposed secrets in public repositories in 2022.
claims.span_hint: section: Pre-commit Hooks, Did You Know callout after custom hook scripts
claims.anchor_text: **Did You Know?** GitHub's secret scanning detected over 700,000 exposed secrets in public repositories in 2022 alone, underscoring the critical need for robust pre-commit security checks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Did You Know?** Public repositories regularly expose large numbers of leaked credentials, which is why automated secret scanning and pre-commit checks matter.
claims.rationale: Official GitHub materials publicly report different 2022 figures, so this exact number should not remain without a verified historical source.

claims.claim_text: Deployments replace failed Pods to maintain the desired number of replicas.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation explicitly states that Deployments replace failed Pods to maintain replica count.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes Job is intended for one-off tasks that run to completion and track successful termination.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation directly defines Jobs this way.

claims.claim_text: Running experiment scripts in a Kubernetes Job guarantees exact hardware and environment parity with production and makes silent failures trivial to diagnose.
claims.span_hint: section: Kubernetes-Based Pipeline Execution, final sentence
claims.anchor_text: By encapsulating deterministic experiment tracking scripts within a Kubernetes `Job`, you guarantee the pipeline executes with the exact hardware and environmental parity of production, making silent failures trivial to diagnose.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Running deterministic experiment or validation workloads as a Kubernetes `Job` can make them easier to reproduce in an environment that is closer to production, which often makes failures easier to diagnose.
claims.rationale: A Kubernetes Job can improve reproducibility, but Kubernetes alone does not guarantee exact production parity or make failures trivial to diagnose.

further_reading.title: MLOps: Continuous delivery and automation pipelines in machine learning
further_reading.why_relevant: A strong high-level reference for ML pipeline maturity, automation, and production operating models.

further_reading.title: DVC: Data Versioning and ML Experiments
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the module's data-versioning and reproducibility sections because it explains DVC's core workflow and pipeline model.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Jobs
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly relevant to the module's guidance on using Jobs instead of Deployments for run-to-completion ML workloads.

notes: Excluded by policy: code blocks, Mermaid diagrams, quiz items, hands-on lab steps, and table rows unless a surrounding prose sentence made a load-bearing factual claim. The Knight Capital, Sculley, and Algorithmia items are real or plausibly real but their cleanest sources sit off allowlist or the module's wording is numerically/date inaccurate.
